summary: Checked 31 claims, found 0 critical mirages. 3 minor/major findings: addon count inaccuracy in Solution section (~27 vs actual 31), Windows memory API naming inconsistency (WorkingSetSize vs Private Working Set), and pseudocode omits constructor calls for addon instantiation. All import paths verified correct. All file paths exist. All API methods confirmed.

findings.claim: Solution section: 'replace DumpMaster (loads ~27 default addons + Dumper + KeepServing + ErrorCheck)'
findings.reality: mitmproxy.addons.default_addons() returns 31 addons, not ~27. DumpMaster also adds ReadFileStdin (omitted from the spec's list). Total with Dumper: 31 + Dumper + KeepServing + ReadFileStdin + ErrorCheck = 35. Decision 1 says '35 default addons' (closer to correct, though it mislabels them all as 'default'). The ~27 figure in the Solution section is off by 4+.
findings.source: tech-spec.md, Solution section (attack vector 1) and Decision 1
findings.fix: Change '~27 default addons' to '31 default addons' in the Solution section. Optionally note that DumpMaster also adds ReadFileStdin beyond the three named extras.

findings.claim: Decision 6 and Acceptance Criteria: 'PROCESS_MEMORY_COUNTERS.WorkingSetSize is stable since Windows XP' paired with 'RAM monitoring triggers at 300MB Private Working Set'
findings.reality: WorkingSetSize in PROCESS_MEMORY_COUNTERS is the Total Working Set (includes shared pages), NOT the Private Working Set. Private Working Set corresponds to PrivateUsage in PROCESS_MEMORY_COUNTERS_EX. For a Python process these values are close, but the spec names them inconsistently — the narrative says 'Private Working Set' while the struct field used is WorkingSetSize (Total Working Set). This is a naming mismatch, not a functional blocker.
findings.fix: Either rename 'Private Working Set' to 'Working Set' throughout, or switch to PROCESS_MEMORY_COUNTERS_EX.PrivateUsage and rename consistently.

findings.claim: Architecture 'How it works' pseudocode: master.addons.add(Core, Proxyserver, NextLayer, TlsConfig) — passes class names without parentheses
findings.reality: mitmproxy AddonManager.add() requires instances, not classes. Correct call is master.addons.add(Core(), Proxyserver(), NextLayer(), TlsConfig()). The pseudocode without () would pass class objects and fail at runtime. This is likely intentional pseudocode shorthand, but a developer following it literally would produce broken code.
findings.source: tech-spec.md, Architecture section, 'How it works' pseudocode block
findings.fix: Add parentheses to all addon names in the pseudocode: Core(), Proxyserver(), NextLayer(), TlsConfig(), KeepServing(), ErrorCheck(), GeoFixAddon, FlowCleanup().

verified_claims: test/test_integration_proxy.py does not exist yet (new file, correct)
Master.shutdown() method exists and is thread-safe
SaveHar maintains internal self.flows list (supports spec claim about flow accumulation)
